claim: Newly arrived rescue galgos consistently show hide-in-corner behavior when approached, taking refuge in the most enclosed available space rather than attempting flight when fully enclosed.
stance_target: galgo behavioral mode when cornered or newly contained is hide/freeze, not fight; this differs from open-space flight response
scope_conditions: galgos at SOS Galgos shelter in Spain, intake period; applies to enclosed kennel context
quoted_support: Conde is still a little fearful of people, and when he first arrived, he would hide away in the corner of his kennel — his safe place.

claim: Galgos with galguero trauma history actively flee from human approach — a galgo with 5 years of hunter exposure 'wanted to run away whenever a person approached him.'
stance_target: galgo with high trauma baseline defaults to active flight from all humans, not just strangers — direct approach by any person triggers evasion
scope_conditions: galgos with extended galguero exposure (5+ years); intake period at rescue shelter
quoted_support: During his first five years with a galguero, he learned very little kindness from humans. Instead, he learned fear — that people could hurt, neglect, and starve him. It is no surprise that when he first arrived, this gorgeous boy wanted to run away whenever a person approached him.

claim: Traumatized galgos in open environments run to the nearest corner on human approach — this pattern (run to corner) is distinct from the open-space flight response and has direct implications for trap placement.
stance_target: galgo containment and trap strategy: corner-seeking behavior means enclosed spaces near walls/fences are preferred refuges, and trap placement should target these
scope_conditions: recently rescued galgos in enclosed/semi-enclosed spaces
quoted_support: When Ludi first arrived, she was terrified of people and would run straight to the corner whenever anyone approached — a heartbreaking sign of the neglect and abuse she must have suffered.

claim: Galgos are universally socially oriented toward other dogs even when severely human-avoidant, making a familiar or calm conspecific an effective lure/bridge for traumatized galgos.
stance_target: conspecific lure is the highest-reliability approach tool for traumatized galgos who refuse all human contact
scope_conditions: all galgo profiles in SOS Galgos database regardless of trauma level
quoted_support: Despite everything she had endured, Turrón is one of the gentlest souls you could ever meet... He is sociable with all dogs and also cat-safe.

claim: SOS Galgos advises always keeping galgos on a lead in public because of their speed and excellent eyesight — and notes galgos do not like to be alone, indicating pack-seeking behavior when lost.
stance_target: lost galgo is likely to seek other dogs or familiar conspecifics; isolation amplifies distress and movement
quoted_support: Galgos run very fast and have excellent eyesight, so it's advisable always to have them on a lead when you're out walking them. In common with other dogs, they do not like to be on their own.

claim: Even young galgos (under 1 year) arrive at shelters with fear already conditioned by galgueros, showing that the trauma baseline is not dependent on adult hunting exposure — it can be established within the first months of life.
stance_target: galgo trauma baseline is present even in very young individuals; cannot use age as a proxy for fear level
scope_conditions: galgos at SOS Galgos shelter; puppies and juveniles with galguero exposure
quoted_support: Shauna is still just a puppy — only around a year old — yet sadly, galgeros had already taught her fear before she even had the chance to grow up. When Shauna arrived, she was terrified of people.
